Machine Learning & Explainability · Case Study

What Drives Medical Insurance Costs?

An end-to-end data science project on 1,337 policyholders — from data cleaning and exploratory analysis through a 58-figure visualization suite to 17 tuned predictive models and SHAP explainability. The goal: predict individual annual charges accurately and transparently, then turn the findings into an actionable pricing strategy.

3.8×
smokers cost vs non-smokers ($32,050 vs $8,441)
R² 0.85
cross-validated accuracy (production model)
$41,558
avg cost of an obese smoker — the priciest segment
17
models built, tuned & honestly evaluated

The Challenge

Insurers live or die by how well they predict cost.

Health insurers must predict what a customer will cost to price policies fairly, flag high-risk members, and target wellness spend. Mispricing cuts both ways — too high loses customers, too low loses money on claims. This project answers three questions from a portfolio of policyholders: what drives cost, can we predict it accurately and transparently, and what should the business do differently?

Pythonpandasscikit-learn XGBoostSHAPmatplotlib / seaborn Explainable AI

What Drives Cost

Five findings, each confirmed by multiple independent methods.

  • Smoking dominates. Smokers average $32,050 vs $8,441 for non-smokers — a 3.8× gap. Smokers are 20.5% of members but ~50% of all claims cost.
  • Smoking × obesity compounds. An obese smoker averages $41,558; an obese non-smoker just $8,856. Obesity barely matters unless the person also smokes — the risk multiplies, it doesn't add.
  • Age is a steady secondary driver (~$267/year), for smokers and non-smokers alike.
  • Region is not a reliable driver — its slight edge disappears under variance-robust tests.
  • Sex is not a driver — no practically meaningful difference.

The Compounding Interaction

The single most important pattern in the data.

A flat "smoker surcharge" misses the real story. Among non-smokers, BMI barely moves cost. Among smokers, charges climb sharply past the obesity threshold (BMI 30) — obese smokers form a distinct, much more expensive tier. This interaction is what separates a good cost model from a naive one.

The Model

17 configurations, tuned and honestly cross-validated.

We built and compared linear models, tree ensembles, SVR, KNN, and stacked/voting ensembles — each tuned with grid search and 5-fold cross-validation, with target leakage carefully controlled.

ModelTest R²CV R²Test RMSETest MAE
SVR (target-scaled)0.9240.847$3,317$1,756
Voting (top 3)0.9240.848$3,316$1,931
Stacking (Linear meta)0.922—$3,352$2,112
Gradient Boosting ★ (production)0.9200.840$3,404$2,108
XGBoost0.9190.844$3,422$2,106
Random Forest0.9180.840$3,440$2,150
Linear / Ridge / Lasso / Elastic-Net~0.897~0.825~$3,840~$2,530
Why Gradient Boosting, and why R² 0.85 not 0.92

The top six models are a statistical tie (CV-mean spread 0.008 vs CV std 0.040), so I chose the model that is accurate, simple, and natively explainable rather than a marginally-higher ensemble. And I quote the cross-validated R² ≈ 0.85 — not the flattering single-split 0.92 — because I measured the split's optimism directly (+0.076). Honest numbers beat impressive ones.

Why It Predicts What It Does

Explainable AI (SHAP) — every prediction traced to dollars.

SHAP assigns each feature a signed dollar contribution to every prediction. The smoking-and-BMI factor alone swings an individual's predicted cost by up to +$25,000, with a visible step-change at BMI 30. Smoking-related features account for 62% of the model's predictive weight; region and sex combined, just 3.6%.

Recommendations & Impact

Insights are useless without action.

  • Reprice on a smoker×BMI tier, not a flat surcharge — today's approach under-prices obese smokers by ~$20,000 each.
  • Fund smoking cessation (largest addressable cost pool) and target weight management at smokers specifically (where it actually pays off).
  • Acquire claims / diagnosis data — the model's worst misses are high-cost non-smokers whose drivers aren't in the current data.
  • Deploy transparently with per-quote SHAP explanations; exclude sex and region as rating factors (fairness + they aren't predictive).
Estimated impact (per 10,000 members, conservative)

Risk-based repricing ~$13.1M (premium adequacy) + smoking cessation ~$1.39M + targeted weight management ~$0.78M  =  ~$15.3M/year. The biggest lever — correct risk-based pricing — costs almost nothing to implement.

View the full code & reports on GitHub →

Methodology & Rigor

What makes these numbers trustworthy.

  • Leakage control: target-encoded features refit on the training fold only.
  • Honest evaluation: stratified split, 5-fold CV, and the test-split optimism measured and reported rather than hidden.
  • Reproducible: seeded, modular scripts; a results registry; persisted models and splits.
  • Intellectual honesty: reported that the ensembles merely tied the best single model, and that cited literature figures (e.g. R²=0.94) did not replicate under leakage-controlled CV.
